topic: Breaking Physics Records: A Study of Human Achievement and Innovation
title: Physics Records and Human Achievement: A Review of Exceptional Feats

lesson.learn: Physics is a fundamental science that governs the behavior of energy, matter, and the universe as a whole. In this lesson, we will examine the physics records set by exceptional individuals, such as Felix Baumgartner, who jumped from 24.7 miles (39.8 kilometers) above the Earth's surface on October 14, 2012, setting a new record for the highest altitude jump in history.
lesson.simpler: Some individuals have achieved remarkable feats by exploiting cutting-edge technologies and understanding the principles of physics. For instance, Ahmed Gabr, an Egyptian freediver, reached a depth of 332.35 meters (1,090.01 feet) on September 18, 2014, setting a new Guinness World Record for the deepest dive ever recorded.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The significance of these achievements lies not only in pushing the boundaries of human potential but also in driving innovation and scientific progress. By exploring the frontiers of what is possible, record-breakers contribute to our understanding of the world and facilitate advancements in fields such as materials science, aerodynamics, and propulsion systems.
lesson.deeperDive: One notable example is Felix Baumgartner's record-breaking jump, which was made possible by the advanced technology developed by Red Bull Stratos. Baumgartner's jump utilized a pressurized suit and a state-of-the-art parachute system designed to slow his descent to a safe altitude. Similarly, Juliane Koepcke's survival in the Peruvian jungle was facilitated by her knowledge of wilderness survival skills, emergency preparedness, and human physiology.
lesson.funFact: Andy Green, a Royal Air Force fighter pilot, set the record for the fastest land speed achieved by a manned vehicle, reaching a speed of 763.03 miles per hour (1,227.98 kilometers per hour) on October 15, 1997, in his British-built jet-powered car, Thrust SSC.

people: + Felix Baumgartner - Skydiver and Red Bull Stratos team member
+ Juliane Koepcke - Wilderness survival expert and plane crash survivor
+ Andy Green - Royal Air Force fighter pilot and Thrust SSC driver
+ Ahmed Gabr - Egyptian freediver and Guinness World Record holder
places: + Red Bull Stratos HQ (New York, USA)
+ Thrust SSC Testing Grounds (Sahara Desert, Morocco)
+ The Guinness World Records HQ (London, UK)
years: + 1964 - Roger Bannister becomes the first person to run a mile in under 4 minutes
+ 1930s - Development of the first jet engines
+ 1997 - Andy Green sets the record for the fastest land speed achieved by a manned vehicle
+ 2012 - Felix Baumgartner sets the record for the highest altitude jump in history
+ 2014 - Ahmed Gabr breaks the deepest dive record
concepts: + Wilderness survival skills and emergency preparedness
+ Record-breaking achievements in speed, altitude, and strength
